STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street• Suite 6:rn • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert P. Ottilie 
Attorney at Law 
444 West C Street. Suite 320 
San Diego, CA 92101 ·3597 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-211 
Dear Mr. Ottilie: 
January 11, 2017 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of Katz & Associates (''K&A") for advice 
regarding the conflicts-of-interest provisions under Government Code Section I 090 et seq.1 The 
Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") is not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (/11 re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), meaning that any advice we provide assumes the 
facts the requester provides to us are accurate. If this is not the case, then our advice could be 
different 
Please note that after forwarding your request to the Attorney General's Office and the San 
Diego District Attorney's Office, we did not receive a written response from either entity. (See 
Section 1097.l(c)(4).) Finally, we are required to advise you that the following advice is not 
admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 
1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit K&A from serving as the communications subcontractor on a 
City contract for engineering services where K&A had previously provided communications 
consulting services to the City? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Performing the consulting services did not enable K&A to exert considerable influence 
over the city's contracting decisions relating to the contract. Therefore, K&A is not a "public 
officer" under Section l 090 and is not subject to its provisions. 
FACTS2 
K&A is a public relations firm that focuses on large public projects. K&A contracts directly 
with government clients and also works as a subcontractor to engineering firms. The engineering 
1 All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 
? Our advice is based not only on the facts you and your client have provided but also those provided by the 
City Attorney's Office. 


File No. A-16-211 
Page No. 2 
finns handle the technical, planning, design, environmental and construction elements of capital 
improvement projects. K&A, as a communications subcontractor, develops communications 
programs for engaging and infonning the community and generating needed feedback during the 
various stages of a project or program. 
The City Council of the City of San Diego will be considering approval of a contract (the 
"Morena Design Contract") with AECOM for the design of the Morena Pump Station and 
Conveyance System (the "Morena Project"), a system that will collect wastewater from existing 
sources in the Morena area and pumping it via a new 14-mile pipeline to a processing plant where it 
will be converted to drinking water. AECOM has selected K&A as the subcontractor to perform the 
"Public Information Program Assistance" part of the Morena Design Contract. City Council 
approval of the Morena Design Contract would include approval of K&A as a subcontractor. 
K&A has worked on two prior contracts involving the San Diego Pure Water Program (the 
"Pure Water Program") which is a program for increasing the City's supply of drinking water by 
recycling water. K&A also provides ongoing services for the Pure Water Program which are 
described in the City's Pure Water San Diego Outreach Governance Plan, as described below. 
The Pure WaterSa11 Diego Pl'ogram -1980 to prel·e11t 
The Pure Water Program, which dates back to the 1980's, is the City's 20-year capital 
program to provide a safe, reliable and local drinking water supply from recycled water. Over the 
last ten years, the city's Public Utilities Department conducted studies associated with potable 
reuse. One study involved the comprehensive evaluation of all viable options to maximize the 
production and use of recycled water available from the city's two reclamation plants. This study 
included a public participation component. K&A was not involved in any of these studies. 
K&A Co11tract to Prepare the Public Outreach Pla11 for the Pure Water Program -March 2014 
toMay2016 
In the fall of 2013, the City issued a stand-alone Request for Proposal (the "RFP") for a 
communications finn to assist city staff in explaining to ratepayers, residents and other stakeholders 
the need for enhancing the City's water supply by converting wastewater to drinking water. K&A, 
the successful bidder, entered into a contract with the city to both prepare and implement, over a 24-
month period, the Pure Water Communication Plan (the "Communication Plan".) The City Council 
approved the contract in the spring of 2014. 
The purpose of the Communication Plan was to identify all issues and tools/messages that 
could be used to reach the goals of the Pure Water Program. The Communication Plan states, in 
pertinent part: 
"Pure Water San Diego features a public outreach and education component 
in order to convey timely, accurate and clear program information to San 
Diego's local leaders, stakeholders and residents, as well as interested parties 
from outside the City. This Communication Plan outlines the strategies and 
tactics that Pure Water San Diego will incorporate to maximize public 


awareness and understanding of the program." 
The Communication Plan: 
File No. A-16-211 
Page No. 3 
• Addresses the varied messages, strategies and tactics to increase awareness and 
acceptability of the Pure Water Program. 
• Identifies issues that affect these goals such as distrust in the government, cultural 
barriers, promotion of environmental responsibility, and education.3 
• Defines activities for public outreach, including education materials and tools, 
business and community outreach, water industry involvement, media and social 
media outreach, speakers bureau, and stakeholder engagement. 
• States that "[I]nformational materials tailored to different audiences will address 
specific concerns and needs, be written for a varying knowledge base about the 
project and will convey important messages in a consistent manner." 
The Communication Plan does not refer to or identify any project-level outreach nor does it 
contain any project-specific information or requirements. You state that K&A's role in preparing 
the Communication Plan was to assist the City in developing messaging to create community 
awareness and understanding of the concept of potable reuse. 
MWH Americas, l11c. Co11tract (the "MWH Colltract'') -November 4, 2014 to November 4, 2019 
In 2015, the City entered into a contract (the "MWH Contract") with MWH Americas, Inc. 
("MWH") to serve as the "As-Needed Engineering Technical Services Consultant for Pure Water 
San Diego Program." K&A was the subcontractor to perform the public outreach provisions of the 
contract. You describe the public outreach provisions of the MWH Contract as a continuation of 
K&A 's work on the K&A Contract. 
The MWH contract provides that upon K&A's completion of the Communication Plan, the 
subcontractor would: 
• Assist City staff in implementing the Public Engagement Plan, 
• Develop strategies to assess public support, 
• Make recommendations to modify the program, and 
• Assist City staff in developing education and outreach materials. 
Like the K&A Contract, the subject of the MWH Contract, including its public education 
provisions, was the overall Pure Water Program and not individual projects. 
l For example, "Key Messages" include: Pure Water San Diego (I) "will provide a safe, reliable and cost­
effective drinking water supply," (2) "provides a locally controlled, drought-proof water supply," and (3) "uses proven 
purification technology and is environmentally friendly." 


Morena Project RFP a11d Desig11 Co11tract 
File No. A-16-211 
Page No. 4 
The City Council will be considering a $14 million contract with AECOM for the design of 
the Morena Pump Station and Conveyance System (the "Morena Project"). The Morena Project 
involves collecting wastewater in the Morena area from various existing sources and then pumping 
the wastewater via a new 14-mile pipeline to a processing plant where it will be converted to 
drinking water. AECOM has selected K&A as the subcontractor to perform the "Public Information 
Program Assistance" part of the contract 
Under the Morena Design Contract, the public outreach subcontractor is required to 
"develop and implement an outreach plan in line with the City's overall Pure Water Plan to support 
and coordinate execution of this project." (Emphasis added.) The public outreach subcontractor 
must provide analysis of the specific public outreach necessary for the location, demographics, and 
other issues that may arise in connection with the Morena Project. 
You state that the Morena Project outreach will address questions such as: does the area 
around the Morena Pump Station have cultural or language issues; are there socio-economic issues 
that need to be addressed; can the messaging focus on environmental responsibility? 
The RFP was prepared by the City without any input from K&A. Specifically, K&A was 
not involved in designing or developing any plans or specifications for the RFP, assisting with the 
drafting of the RFP, or participating in any preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 
reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, or solicitation for bids. In addition, the 
Communication Plan itself does not contain any designs or definitions of any project-specific or 
project-level outreach. 
You note that the outreach provisions in the RFP were prepared by the City using standard 
language from prior requests for proposals. The identical language, down to the same numbering of 
provisions describing deliverables, was used as far back as 1996 in a request for proposal for a 
Repurified Water Conveyance project. The provisions (Identified as Task 3 - Public Information 
Program Assistance), require attending a specified number of public meetings, identifying focus 
groups, community groups and impacted communities, scheduling focus group meetings and 
community group meetings and drafting minutes and preparing exhibits and drawings for public 
meetings. K&A was not involved in the 1996 project. 
Pure WaterSa11 Diego Outreach Gover11a11ce Pla11 - Updated J1111e 2016 
As part of the Pure Water Program, the City prepared the Pure Water Outreach Governance 
Plan, an internal document that contains processes for streamlining communications among the 
outreach activities of Pure Water Program staff/consultants and project staff/consultants. Under the 
Outreach Governance Plan, Ms. Katz serves as a member of a "Program-Level Outreach" team. In 
that role, she assists Brent Eidson, the Public Utilities Department's Outreach Lead, in overseeing 
project-specific outreach. However, the Morena Project is not among the projects Ms. Katz will 
oversee. 


File No. A-16-211 
Page No. 5 
You ask whether, under Section 1090, K&A's prior work on the Pure Water Program 
precludes K&A serving as the public outreach subcontractor on the Morena Design Contract. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities from 
making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with financial 
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies.4) Section 
l 090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety."5 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest."6 A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 7 
The term "public officer" is interpreted broadly under Section 1090 and includes 
"independent contractors who perform a public function" and "whose official capacities carry the 
potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency."8 The 
purpose of this broad definition is to ensure that independent contractors who are essentially 
performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same "fealty expected from 
permanent officers and employees. "9 
"Participating in the making or forming of a contract," is also defined broadly in the Section 
l 090 context, and includes involvement in preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 
reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. 10 
The threshold question before us is whether K&A is subject to the provisions of Section 
l 090. A corporate consultant working as an independent contractor for a public entity may be an 
"employee" for purposes of civil liability under Section 1090 if the corporate consultant served as a 
4 Stiga/11•. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565,569. 
5 City of Imperial Beach 1 •• Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197. 
6 (People 1•. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289,333 
1 Thomson 1•.Ca/1 (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649. 
8 (See Hub City, s11pm, 186 Cal.App.4111 at p. 124-1125; citing California Housing Finance Agency,,_ 
Hm1ol'er/California Management & Accounting Center, Inc. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-693; see also Dal'is 1·. 
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, at pp. 300-301.) 
9 46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965). 
10 (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Sw,•fral l'. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also 
Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 569.) 


File No. A-16-21 l 
Page No. 6 
professional consultant to the public entity and "had a hand in designing and developing the plans 
and specifications" for a project and then seeks the contract for implementing those plans and 
specifications.1 1  
To detennine whether Section 1090 applies, the focus is on the scope of influence the 
corporate consultant exerts in advising the public entity.12 Section I 090 applies to a corporate 
consultant that has been delegated governmental responsibilities that carry the potential to exert 
considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public entity.13 A corporate consultant 
that plays a limited technical role and is more removed from influencing the contracting decisions 
of a public agency may not be subject to Section 1090. 14 Therefore, we must detennine whether 
K&A had been delegated governmental responsibilities which enabled K&A to exert considerable 
influence in designing and developing the plans and specifications for the RFP and Morena Design 
Contract. You have asked us to apply the standards set forth in the Chadwick Advice Letter No. A-
15-14 7 in making this determination. (We will henceforth refer to the advice letter as "Clzadwick-
1. ") 
Chadwick-} involved a city contract for landscape architecture services for redesigning and 
improvements to the Torrey Pines North Golf Course. Schmidt Design Group ("Schmidt") served 
as the prime landscape architectural consultant. The scope of work included: 
• Developing the preliminary design for the project, called the General Development 
Plan (the "GDP"), 
• Presenting the GDP to various City committees, 
• Processing the Coastal Development Pennit, 
• Assisting with the preparation of the Mitigating Negative Declaration, 
• Providing detailed graphics for the landscape development package, 
• Preparing preliminary planting plans, and 
• Providing detailed cost elements. 
As the subconsultant, Langardere Unlimited: 
• Worked on developing the GDP, 
• Provided detailed site analysis, 
• Researched golf course playability, 
• Reviewed view corridors, 
1 1 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 50 (2006); see Fr"ser-Y"mor Age11cy, Inc. 1·. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 
Cal.App.3d 201, 21 l-212.)1 1  Page Advice Letter, No. A-16-044 citing to D(ll'is, supra, 237 Cal.App.4th 261 al p. 301; 
see Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084. 
1 Green, supra; see Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A• 15-147. 
ll See Green, supra; P"ge, s11pm; Fowler Advice Letter, No. A• 15-228; Chadwick, supra; see also Hub City 
Solid Waste Sen·ice. Inc ,,. City of Compton (2010) l 86 Cal.App.4th 114, 1 124-1125; Schaefer I'. Berinstein ( 1956) 140 
Cal.App.2d 278, 291. 
1 See Green, supra; Chadwick, supra. 


File No. A-16-21 1 
Page No. 7 
• 
Participated in community meetings and attended the City's various review boards 
and commissions, 
• 
Prepared cost estimates. 
• Coordinated and collaborated with Schmidt, the City's project manager and other 
City staff to develop the GDP and create the final design. 
When the City later issued the request for proposal, using Schmidt's design and research 
efforts, as well as the GDP that Schmidt had worked on with City staff, Schmidt wished to bid on 
the request for proposal, using Langadere as the subcontractor. The request for proposal was for 
rebuilding fairway and greenside bunkers, reconstructing or redesigning greens, leveling and 
constructing tees, and replacing the irrigation system. 
We found that Schmidt "was in a position to interact with and advise the City on its policy 
goals, create a design that interprets and applies the City's stated plan for the golf course, and work 
closely with the project manager and other staff to ensure the City and community supported the 
design." In this role, Schmidt and Langardere had the capability of exerting influence over the City 
staffs decisionmaking and, therefore, were subject to Section 1090. 
Subsequent advice letters have considered circumstances similar to those in Chadwick-] in 
determining that a contractor's work under a prior contract precluded the contractor from bidding 
on a subsequent contract. 
In the Fowler Advice Letter, No. A-15-228, a contractor was hired to review the city's 
development impact fees in preparation for the city's proposed update of the fees. The work 
included: 
• Identifying, evaluating and recommending alternative funding options, 
• 
Making technical recommendations for the fee update process, 
• Teaching city councilmembers about the city's development impact fees, 
• Performing nexus studies, and 
• 
Evaluating the fees and studies. 
• 
Working closely with city staff in providing community stakeholders the opportunity 
to provide input on the fee update process. 
We found that the contractor was prohibited from bidding on the subsequent request for proposal 
for updating the fees because the contractor was intricately involved in the development and 
formation of the subsequent contract through its performance of the prior contract. 
In a more recent advice letter, also issued to Mr. Chadwick, A-16-090, ("Chadwick-2"), we 
found that the prime contractor for a contract to develop the city's master plan for a traffic signal 
communications system was prohibited from contracting with the city to provide as-needed 
consulting services that we said was based on the plan. The master plan identified system 
deficiencies and contained almost 50 pages of suggestions and recommendations for upgrading 
traffic signal controllers, fiber optic multiplexes, wireless equipment, etc. We found that because 
the contractor was integrally involved in the city 's adoption of the master plan, both contracts 


File No. A-16-211 
Page No. 8 
covered the same subject and the second contract implemented some of the proposals in the master 
plan, the master plan essentially described the scope of service for the second contract. 
Unlike the facts in Chadwick-), K&A did not "create a design" for any project, including 
the Morena Project, that would become the subject of the su"bsequent RFP. Unlike the facts in 
Fowler, the Communication Plan does not essentially describe the scope of service for the RFP. 
Unlike Fowler and Chadwick-2, K&A's prior work did not consist of identifying deficiencies in a 
system, making recommendations for rectifying those deficiencies and then bidding on a contract to 
implement those recommendations. 
Also, in each of the three advice letters, the subject matter of the first and subsequent 
contract was the same - the redesign of the golf course (Chadwick-)), upgrading the traffic signal 
system (Chadwick-2) and updating development impact fees (Fowler). Under the facts here, the 
subject of the two contracts is not the same. The subject of the Communication Plan is the Pure 
Water Program - a capital program to provide a safe, reliable and local drinking water supply from 
recycled water. The subject of the public outreach provisions of the Morena Design Contract is to 
identify and inform the Morena community on the possible impacts of the Morena Project on them. 
In our review of the Communication Plan, we found it apparent that the very nature of the 
messaging created by K&A limits its use in project-specific public outreach to increasing awareness 
and acceptability of recycling the city's waste water. 
Specifically, the RFP and Morena Design Contract require the subcontractor to: 
• Identify focus groups, community groups and communities impacted by the Moreno 
Project pipeline and pumping station, 
• 
Schedule meetings for these groups and other community groups, 
• Attend a specified number of public information meetings for a specified number of 
hours, and 
• Draft meeting minutes and prepare exhibits and drawings for the meetings. 
These provisions come from a standard form of requests for proposals used by the City in the past. 
The provisions are specific to the actual neighborhood of a project and focus on informing people in 
that specific community of the project's impacts on them. 
Based on the facts you have provided and our review of the documents, we find that K&A 
did not exert considerable influence over the city's contracting decisions relating to the Morena 
Design Contract. Therefore, the City is not prohibited from entering into the Morena Design 
Contract with AECOM which includes K&A as the public outreach subcontractor. 
Political Reform Act 
Under Section 87100, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public 
official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her positon to influence a governmental 
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the 
official's financial interests. 


File No. A-1 6-21 1 
Page No. 9 
You have not inquired regarding any potential conflicts of interest that may arise under the 
Act. However, the language in the Outreach Governance Plan suggests that Ms. Katz may have a 
conflict of interest in decisions she may make or participate in making in her role as a co-outreach 
lead with oversight of project-specific outreach. This would be the case if Ms. Katz is considered to 
be a "consultant" as defined in Regulation 18700.3 and, as a consultant, makes, participates in 
making or attempts to use her position to influence a governmental decision involving the Morena 
Project. You state that neither Ms. Katz nor K&A has any oversight role in the Morena Project's 
public outreach. So long as Ms. Katz will not in the future make, participate in making, or use her 
position as a consultant to influence a governmental decision involving the Morena Project, there 
will be no conflict of interest under the Act. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
-tWw

By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit K&A from serving as the communications subcontractor on a 
City contract for engineering services where K&A had previously provided communications 
consulting services to the City?
sections.conclusion:
No. Performing the consulting services did not enable K&A to exert considerable influence 
over the city's contracting decisions relating to the contract. Therefore, K&A is not a "public 
officer" under Section l 090 and is not subject to its provisions.

K&A is a public relations firm that focuses on large public projects. K&A contracts directly 
with government clients and also works as a subcontractor to engineering firms. The engineering

• Assist City staff in implementing the Public Engagement Plan,

Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities from 
making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with financial 
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies.4) Section 
l 090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety."5 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest."6 A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 7 
The term "public officer" is interpreted broadly under Section 1090 and includes 
"independent contractors who perform a public function" and "whose official capacities carry the 
potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency."8 The 
purpose of this broad definition is to ensure that independent contractors who are essentially 
performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same "fealty expected from 
permanent officers and employees. "9 
"Participating in the making or forming of a contract," is also defined broadly in the Section 
l 090 context, and includes involvement in preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 
reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. 10 
The threshold question before us is whether K&A is subject to the provisions of Section 
l 090. A corporate consultant working as an independent contractor for a public entity may be an 
"employee" for purposes of civil liability under Section 1090 if the corporate consultant served as a 
4 Stiga/11•. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565,569. 
5 City of Imperial Beach 1 •• Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197. 
6 (People 1•. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289,333 
1 Thomson 1•.Ca/1 (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649. 
8 (See Hub City, s11pm, 186 Cal.App.4111 at p. 124-1125; citing California Housing Finance Agency,,_ 
Hm1ol'er/California Management & Accounting Center, Inc. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-693; see also Dal'is 1·. 
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, at pp. 300-301.) 
9 46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965). 
10 (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Sw,•fral l'. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also 
Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 569.) 


File No. A-16-21 l  
professional consultant to the public entity and "had a hand in designing and developing the plans 
and specifications" for a project and then seeks the contract for implementing those plans and 
specifications.1 1  
To detennine whether Section 1090 applies, the focus is on the scope of influence the 
corporate consultant exerts in advising the public entity.12 Section I 090 applies to a corporate 
consultant that has been delegated governmental responsibilities that carry the potential to exert 
considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public entity.13 A corporate consultant 
that plays a limited technical role and is more removed from influencing the contracting decisions 
of a public agency may not be subject to Section 1090. 14 Therefore, we must detennine whether 
K&A had been delegated governmental responsibilities which enabled K&A to exert considerable 
influence in designing and developing the plans and specifications for the RFP and Morena Design 
Contract. You have asked us to apply the standards set forth in the Chadwick Advice Letter No. A-
15-14 7 in making this determination. (We will henceforth refer to the advice letter as "Clzadwick-
1. ") 
Chadwick-} involved a city contract for landscape architecture services for redesigning and 
improvements to the Torrey Pines North Golf Course. Schmidt Design Group ("Schmidt") served 
as the prime landscape architectural consultant. The scope of work included: 
• Developing the preliminary design for the project, called the General Development 
Plan (the "GDP"), 
• Presenting the GDP to various City committees, 
• Processing the Coastal Development Pennit, 
• Assisting with the preparation of the Mitigating Negative Declaration, 
• Providing detailed graphics for the landscape development package, 
• Preparing preliminary planting plans, and 
• Providing detailed cost elements. 
As the subconsultant, Langardere Unlimited: 
• Worked on developing the GDP, 
• Provided detailed site analysis, 
• Researched golf course playability, 
• Reviewed view corridors, 
1 1 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 50 (2006); see Fr"ser-Y"mor Age11cy, Inc. 1·. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 
Cal.App.3d 201, 21 l-212.)1 1  Page Advice Letter, No. A-16-044 citing to D(ll'is, supra, 237 Cal.App.4th 261 al p. 301; 
see Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084. 
1 Green, supra; see Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A• 15-147. 
ll See Green, supra; P"ge, s11pm; Fowler Advice Letter, No. A• 15-228; Chadwick, supra; see also Hub City 
Solid Waste Sen·ice. Inc ,,. City of Compton (2010) l 86 Cal.App.4th 114, 1 124-1125; Schaefer I'. Berinstein ( 1956) 140 
Cal.App.2d 278, 291. 
1 See Green, supra; Chadwick, supra. 


File No. A-16-21 1  
• 
Participated in community meetings and attended the City's various review boards 
and commissions, 
• 
Prepared cost estimates. 
• Coordinated and collaborated with Schmidt, the City's project manager and other 
City staff to develop the GDP and create the final design. 
When the City later issued the request for proposal, using Schmidt's design and research 
efforts, as well as the GDP that Schmidt had worked on with City staff, Schmidt wished to bid on 
the request for proposal, using Langadere as the subcontractor. The request for proposal was for 
rebuilding fairway and greenside bunkers, reconstructing or redesigning greens, leveling and 
constructing tees, and replacing the irrigation system. 
We found that Schmidt "was in a position to interact with and advise the City on its policy 
goals, create a design that interprets and applies the City's stated plan for the golf course, and work 
closely with the project manager and other staff to ensure the City and community supported the 
design." In this role, Schmidt and Langardere had the capability of exerting influence over the City 
staffs decisionmaking and, therefore, were subject to Section 1090. 
Subsequent advice letters have considered circumstances similar to those in Chadwick-] in 
determining that a contractor's work under a prior contract precluded the contractor from bidding 
on a subsequent contract. 
In the Fowler Advice Letter, No. A-15-228, a contractor was hired to review the city's 
development impact fees in preparation for the city's proposed update of the fees. The work 
included: 
• Identifying, evaluating and recommending alternative funding options, 
• 
Making technical recommendations for the fee update process, 
• Teaching city councilmembers about the city's development impact fees, 
• Performing nexus studies, and 
• 
Evaluating the fees and studies. 
• 
Working closely with city staff in providing community stakeholders the opportunity 
to provide input on the fee update process. 
We found that the contractor was prohibited from bidding on the subsequent request for proposal 
for updating the fees because the contractor was intricately involved in the development and 
formation of the subsequent contract through its performance of the prior contract. 
In a more recent advice letter, also issued to Mr. Chadwick, A-16-090, ("Chadwick-2"), we 
found that the prime contractor for a contract to develop the city's master plan for a traffic signal 
communications system was prohibited from contracting with the city to provide as-needed 
consulting services that we said was based on the plan. The master plan identified system 
deficiencies and contained almost 50 pages of suggestions and recommendations for upgrading 
traffic signal controllers, fiber optic multiplexes, wireless equipment, etc. We found that because 
the contractor was integrally involved in the city 's adoption of the master plan, both contracts  
covered the same subject and the second contract implemented some of the proposals in the master 
plan, the master plan essentially described the scope of service for the second contract. 
Unlike the facts in Chadwick-), K&A did not "create a design" for any project, including 
the Morena Project, that would become the subject of the su"bsequent RFP. Unlike the facts in 
Fowler, the Communication Plan does not essentially describe the scope of service for the RFP. 
Unlike Fowler and Chadwick-2, K&A's prior work did not consist of identifying deficiencies in a 
system, making recommendations for rectifying those deficiencies and then bidding on a contract to 
implement those recommendations. 
Also, in each of the three advice letters, the subject matter of the first and subsequent 
contract was the same - the redesign of the golf course (Chadwick-)), upgrading the traffic signal 
system (Chadwick-2) and updating development impact fees (Fowler). Under the facts here, the 
subject of the two contracts is not the same. The subject of the Communication Plan is the Pure 
Water Program - a capital program to provide a safe, reliable and local drinking water supply from 
recycled water. The subject of the public outreach provisions of the Morena Design Contract is to 
identify and inform the Morena community on the possible impacts of the Morena Project on them. 
In our review of the Communication Plan, we found it apparent that the very nature of the 
messaging created by K&A limits its use in project-specific public outreach to increasing awareness 
and acceptability of recycling the city's waste water. 
Specifically, the RFP and Morena Design Contract require the subcontractor to: 
• Identify focus groups, community groups and communities impacted by the Moreno 
Project pipeline and pumping station, 
• 
Schedule meetings for these groups and other community groups, 
• Attend a specified number of public information meetings for a specified number of 
hours, and 
• Draft meeting minutes and prepare exhibits and drawings for the meetings. 
These provisions come from a standard form of requests for proposals used by the City in the past. 
The provisions are specific to the actual neighborhood of a project and focus on informing people in 
that specific community of the project's impacts on them. 
Based on the facts you have provided and our review of the documents, we find that K&A 
did not exert considerable influence over the city's contracting decisions relating to the Morena 
Design Contract. Therefore, the City is not prohibited from entering into the Morena Design 
Contract with AECOM which includes K&A as the public outreach subcontractor. 
Political Reform Act 
Under Section 87100, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public 
official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her positon to influence a governmental 
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the 
official's financial interests. 


File No. A-1 6-21 1  
You have not inquired regarding any potential conflicts of interest that may arise under the 
Act. However, the language in the Outreach Governance Plan suggests that Ms. Katz may have a 
conflict of interest in decisions she may make or participate in making in her role as a co-outreach 
lead with oversight of project-specific outreach. This would be the case if Ms. Katz is considered to 
be a "consultant" as defined in Regulation 18700.3 and, as a consultant, makes, participates in 
making or attempts to use her position to influence a governmental decision involving the Morena 
Project. You state that neither Ms. Katz nor K&A has any oversight role in the Morena Project's 
public outreach. So long as Ms. Katz will not in the future make, participate in making, or use her 
position as a consultant to influence a governmental decision involving the Morena Project, there 
will be no conflict of interest under the Act.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prohibit K&A from serving as the communications subcontractor on a 
City contract for engineering services where K&A had previously provided communications 
consulting services to the City?

CONCLUSION: No. Performing the consulting services did not enable K&A to exert considerable influence 
over the city's contracting decisions relating to the contract. Therefore, K&A is not a "public 
officer" under Section l 090 and is not subject to its provisions. 
FACTS2 
K&A is a public relations firm that focuses on large public projects. K&A contracts directly 
with government clients and also works as a subcontractor to engineering firms. The engineering 
1 All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 
? Our advice is based not only on the facts you and your client have provided but also those provided by the 
City Attorney's Office.  
finns handle the technical, planning, design, environmental and construction elements of capital 
improvement projects. K&A, as a communications subcontractor, develops communications 
programs for engaging and infonning the community and generating needed feedback during the 
various stages of a project or program. 
The City Council of the City of San Diego will be considering approval of a contract (the 
"Morena Design Contract") with AECOM for the design of the Morena Pump Station and 
Conveyance System (the "Morena Project"), a system that will collect wastewater from existing 
sources in the Morena area and pumping it via a new 14-mile pipeline to a processing plant where it 
will be converted to drinking water. AECOM has selected K&A as the subcontractor to perform the 
"Public Information Program Assistance" part of the Morena Design Contract. City Council 
approval of the Morena Design Contract would include approval of K&A as a subcontractor. 
K&A has worked on two prior contracts involving the San Diego Pure Water Program (the 
"Pure Water Program") which is a program for increasing the City's supply of drinking water by 
recycling water. K&A also provides ongoing services for the Pure Water Program which are 
described in the City's Pure Water San Diego Outreach Governance Plan, as described below. 
The Pure WaterSa11 Diego Pl'ogram -1980 to prel·e11t 
The Pure Water Program, which dates back to the 1980's, is the City's 20-year capital 
program to provide a safe, reliable and local drinking water supply from recycled water. Over the 
last ten years, the city's Public Utilities Department conducted studies associated with potable 
reuse. One study involved the comprehensive evaluation of all viable options to maximize the 
production and use of recycled water available from the city's two reclamation plants. This study 
included a public participation component. K&A was not involved in any of these studies. 
K&A Co11tract to Prepare the Public Outreach Pla11 for the Pure Water Program -March 2014 
toMay2016 
In the fall of 2013, the City issued a stand-alone Request for Proposal (the "RFP") for a 
communications finn to assist city staff in explaining to ratepayers, residents and other stakeholders 
the need for enhancing the City's water supply by converting wastewater to drinking water. K&A, 
the successful bidder, entered into a contract with the city to both prepare and implement, over a 24-
month period, the Pure Water Communication Plan (the "Communication Plan".) The City Council 
approved the contract in the spring of 2014. 
The purpose of the Communication Plan was to identify all issues and tools/messages that 
could be used to reach the goals of the Pure Water Program. The Communication Plan states, in 
pertinent part: 
"Pure Water San Diego features a public outreach and education component 
in order to convey timely, accurate and clear program information to San 
Diego's local leaders, stakeholders and residents, as well as interested parties 
from outside the City. This Communication Plan outlines the strategies and 
tactics that Pure Water San Diego will incorporate to maximize public 


awareness and understanding of the program." 
The Communication Plan:  
• Addresses the varied messages, strategies and tactics to increase awareness and 
acceptability of the Pure Water Program. 
• Identifies issues that affect these goals such as distrust in the government, cultural 
barriers, promotion of environmental responsibility, and education.3 
• Defines activities for public outreach, including education materials and tools, 
business and community outreach, water industry involvement, media and social 
media outreach, speakers bureau, and stakeholder engagement. 
• States that "[I]nformational materials tailored to different audiences will address 
specific concerns and needs, be written for a varying knowledge base about the 
project and will convey important messages in a consistent manner." 
The Communication Plan does not refer to or identify any project-level outreach nor does it 
contain any project-specific information or requirements. You state that K&A's role in preparing 
the Communication Plan was to assist the City in developing messaging to create community 
awareness and understanding of the concept of potable reuse. 
MWH Americas, l11c. Co11tract (the "MWH Colltract'') -November 4, 2014 to November 4, 2019 
In 2015, the City entered into a contract (the "MWH Contract") with MWH Americas, Inc. 
("MWH") to serve as the "As-Needed Engineering Technical Services Consultant for Pure Water 
San Diego Program." K&A was the subcontractor to perform the public outreach provisions of the 
contract. You describe the public outreach provisions of the MWH Contract as a continuation of 
K&A 's work on the K&A Contract. 
The MWH contract provides that upon K&A's completion of the Communication Plan, the 
subcontractor would: 
• Assist City staff in implementing the Public Engagement Plan, 
• Develop strategies to assess public support, 
• Make recommendations to modify the program, and 
• Assist City staff in developing education and outreach materials. 
Like the K&A Contract, the subject of the MWH Contract, including its public education 
provisions, was the overall Pure Water Program and not individual projects. 
l For example, "Key Messages" include: Pure Water San Diego (I) "will provide a safe, reliable and cost­
effective drinking water supply," (2) "provides a locally controlled, drought-proof water supply," and (3) "uses proven 
purification technology and is environmentally friendly." 


Morena Project RFP a11d Desig11 Co11tract  
The City Council will be considering a $14 million contract with AECOM for the design of 
the Morena Pump Station and Conveyance System (the "Morena Project"). The Morena Project 
involves collecting wastewater in the Morena area from various existing sources and then pumping 
the wastewater via a new 14-mile pipeline to a processing plant where it will be converted to 
drinking water. AECOM has selected K&A as the subcontractor to perform the "Public Information 
Program Assistance" part of the contract 
Under the Morena Design Contract, the public outreach subcontractor is required to 
"develop and implement an outreach plan in line with the City's overall Pure Water Plan to support 
and coordinate execution of this project." (Emphasis added.) The public outreach subcontractor 
must provide analysis of the specific public outreach necessary for the location, demographics, and 
other issues that may arise in connection with the Morena Project. 
You state that the Morena Project outreach will address questions such as: does the area 
around the Morena Pump Station have cultural or language issues; are there socio-economic issues 
that need to be addressed; can the messaging focus on environmental responsibility? 
The RFP was prepared by the City without any input from K&A. Specifically, K&A was 
not involved in designing or developing any plans or specifications for the RFP, assisting with the 
drafting of the RFP, or participating in any preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 
reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, or solicitation for bids. In addition, the 
Communication Plan itself does not contain any designs or definitions of any project-specific or 
project-level outreach. 
You note that the outreach provisions in the RFP were prepared by the City using standard 
language from prior requests for proposals. The identical language, down to the same numbering of 
provisions describing deliverables, was used as far back as 1996 in a request for proposal for a 
Repurified Water Conveyance project. The provisions (Identified as Task 3 - Public Information 
Program Assistance), require attending a specified number of public meetings, identifying focus 
groups, community groups and impacted communities, scheduling focus group meetings and 
community group meetings and drafting minutes and preparing exhibits and drawings for public 
meetings. K&A was not involved in the 1996 project. 
Pure WaterSa11 Diego Outreach Gover11a11ce Pla11 - Updated J1111e 2016 
As part of the Pure Water Program, the City prepared the Pure Water Outreach Governance 
Plan, an internal document that contains processes for streamlining communications among the 
outreach activities of Pure Water Program staff/consultants and project staff/consultants. Under the 
Outreach Governance Plan, Ms. Katz serves as a member of a "Program-Level Outreach" team. In 
that role, she assists Brent Eidson, the Public Utilities Department's Outreach Lead, in overseeing 
project-specific outreach. However, the Morena Project is not among the projects Ms. Katz will 
oversee.  
You ask whether, under Section 1090, K&A's prior work on the Pure Water Program 
precludes K&A serving as the public outreach subcontractor on the Morena Design Contract.

Ottilie Attorney at Law 444 West C Street. Suite 320 San Diego, CA 92101 ·3597 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-211 Dear Mr. Ottilie: January 11, 2017 This letter responds to your request on behalf of Katz & Associates (''K&A") for advice regarding the conflicts-of-interest provisions under Government Code Section I 090 et seq.1 The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") is not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), meaning that any advice we provide assumes the facts the requester provides to us are accurate. If this is not the case, then our advice could be different Please note that after forwarding your request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego District Attorney's Office, we did not receive a written response from either entity. (See Section 1097.l(c)(4).) Finally, we are required to advise you that the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prohibit K&A from serving as the communications subcontractor on a City contract for engineering services where K&A had previously provided communications consulting services to the City? CONCLUSION No. Performing the consulting services did not enable K&A to exert considerable influence over the city's contracting decisions relating to the contract. Therefore, K&A is not a "public officer" under Section l 090 and is not subject to its provisions. FACTS2 K&A is a public relations firm that focuses on large public projects. K&A contracts directly with government clients and also works as a subcontractor to engineering firms. The engineering 1 All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. ?
